caveats: a: BLS publishes an occupation mix for NAICS 561500, not Convention and Visitors Bureaus alone, and that parent is dominated by travel agencies and reservation services.
a: Destinations International describes functions and examples but does not report a wage-weighted task inventory or current automation baseline.
a: Public-facing marketing workflows may be more exposed than convention sales, stakeholder governance, and destination-development work, so organization mix matters materially.
rho: The 2025 Futures Study has 537 participants across 36 countries rather than a representative U.S. six-digit sample.
rho: The industry blog emphasizes a handful of named adopters and does not publish the survey response count or realized payroll savings.
rho: Public records, accessibility, procurement, brand safety, and community-trust obligations can slow implementation even when tools are technically available.
e: No source measures the legal-form and transferability distribution of the 101 frozen LMM-band firms.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a commercial-services margin that may be misleading for nonprofit and budget-consuming destination organizations.

e: Public funding does not by itself prove public ownership, and some nonprofit organizations contract with governments while remaining operationally independent, but neither structure creates ordinary equity control.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Destination-organization governance changes, executive succession, mergers of nonprofits, and public contract rebids usually are not purchases of equity control.
s5: The tiny eligible subset makes deal-frequency estimates especially unstable.
q: No source measures the five-year share of implemented gross benefit retained by U.S. lower-middle-market CVBs.
q: Retention as distributable margin differs sharply from retention as additional mission capacity in nonprofit organizations.
q: Funding structures vary among dedicated taxes, improvement-district assessments, municipal contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants.
d5: Travel spending is a demand driver, not a direct measure of CVB service quantity or constant-quality output.
d5: The forecast extends through 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026, and travel forecasts can change materially.
d5: Funding risk is global survey evidence, while travel forecasts are national U.S. aggregates that mask destination-specific winners and losers.
o: No source quantifies the operator-required share for CVBs over five years.
o: AI-powered search is already reducing traditional website traffic, but its effect on convention wins, partner value, and public mandates is not measured.
o: Large convention destinations and small visitor-information bureaus have different exposure to self-service substitution.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The economic customers and funders are commonly municipalities, tourism improvement districts, lodging and hospitality members, and other destination stakeholders, while travelers and meeting planners receive many services without a direct charge. Revenue commonly comes from dedicated occupancy or sales taxes, district assessments, government service contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants rather than transaction fees from visitors.
lens.excluded_activities: Government tourism-development offices, captive municipal departments, entities without independently transferable control, shells, convention and trade-show organizers, venue operators, travel agencies, tour operators, and software-only travel-information products.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators repeatedly providing destination marketing, convention and meeting sales support, visitor information, itinerary content, local-partner promotion, and related destination-management services to external municipalities, tourism districts, lodging stakeholders, event organizers, businesses, and travelers.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports employment shares and wages for the broader NAICS 561500 parent, while Destinations International documents destination-organization workflows and current AI uses. Transformation: the parent occupation mix is reweighted conceptually toward CVB marketing, research, sales, visitor-information, and administrative work, then the not-yet-automated portions of content production, research synthesis, inquiry handling, localization, CRM upkeep, reporting, and sales preparation are mapped to wage-weighted exposure. Direction of error: the parent industry's large travel-agent and reservation occupations likely overstate routine transactional exposure, while its very small published creative share likely understates CVB-specific marketing and content work; current unmeasured tool use may make residual exposure lower.
caveats: BLS publishes an occupation mix for NAICS 561500, not Convention and Visitors Bureaus alone, and that parent is dominated by travel agencies and reservation services.
Destinations International describes functions and examples but does not report a wage-weighted task inventory or current automation baseline.
Public-facing marketing workflows may be more exposed than convention sales, stakeholder governance, and destination-development work, so organization mix matters materially.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-and-time study from Destinations International's reporting platform that separates destination marketing, content, convention sales, visitor services, research, partner relations, advocacy, finance, and already-automated work would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: CVB work contains a large digital information and marketing layer that AI can draft, translate, summarize, personalize, classify, and analyze. Exposure is constrained by convention selling, government and board relations, local-partner coordination, destination strategy, community engagement, brand judgment, and on-site visitor or event work that still depend on accountable people and local knowledge.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: U.S. Travel Association forecasts inflation-adjusted travel spending, including domestic leisure, business, and group travel, while Destinations International measures funding risk and expanding destination-organization mandates. Transformation: forecast real travel activity through 2030 is used as a demand anchor and tempered for the weaker link between visitor spending and bureau budgets, international volatility, public-funding diversion, and local political choices to estimate year-five constant-price demand for the current CVB service basket. Direction of error: national travel spending growth may overstate bureau demand where funding is cut or marketing shifts to platforms, but may understate demand where organizations add destination development, events, community engagement, and data responsibilities.
caveats: Travel spending is a demand driver, not a direct measure of CVB service quantity or constant-quality output.
The forecast extends through 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026, and travel forecasts can change materially.
Funding risk is global survey evidence, while travel forecasts are national U.S. aggregates that mask destination-specific winners and losers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of constant-dollar U.S. destination-organization budgets, service volumes, convention leads, bookings, visitor inquiries, funding cuts, and mandate expansions, matched to local travel demand, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: The spring 2026 forecast shows continued real growth in domestic leisure, business, and group travel through 2030, supporting ongoing destination promotion and convention work. Demand for bureau services is less secure than travel demand itself: 42 percent of destination organizations reported near-term funding at risk, international demand remains volatile, and public budgets can diverge from visitor volumes. Expanded mandates partly offset those risks.

caveats: No source measures the legal-form and transferability distribution of the 101 frozen LMM-band firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a commercial-services margin that may be misleading for nonprofit and budget-consuming destination organizations.

Public funding does not by itself prove public ownership, and some nonprofit organizations contract with governments while remaining operationally independent, but neither structure creates ordinary equity control.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm audit of the frozen population using IRS status, secretary-of-state records, municipal contracts, governing documents, beneficial ownership, funding concentration, change-of-control clauses, and normalized distributable earnings would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The activity is recurring and externally oriented, but the transferable-firm subset is unusually narrow. Destinations International reports that 92 percent of destination-organization funding comes through public-sector channels, and a large operating example, Visit Denver, is a private nonprofit governed by a board and contracted by the city. Many bureaus are therefore public, quasi-public, nonprofit, or contract-dependent organizations whose control cannot be purchased like an ordinary company. The eligible minority consists of genuinely private, independently transferable operators with durable service contracts, stakeholder relationships, and economics separable from a public body or nonprofit charter.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561591 ($711,103,647) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561591 ($2,683,601,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $335,408 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561591); class <5: 498 firms, avg revenue/firm $411,076 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,333 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 189 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,572,005 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $246,019 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,504,186 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $548,405 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,938,849 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,650,930 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,952,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,218,066 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 101

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: Census defines the CVB service basket, and Destinations International documents both self-service AI capabilities and the industry's expanding coordination, advocacy, destination-development, brand, event, and community roles. Transformation: routine information, itinerary, translation, content, search, and first-line inquiry volumes are treated as increasingly self-served, while convention representation, official brand accountability, local partnerships, funding advocacy, community alignment, and destination stewardship remain operator-required. Direction of error: rapid AI-agent adoption could disintermediate more visitor discovery and meeting research than assumed, while the industry's quasi-public mandate and local convening role could preserve more operator-required demand.
caveats: No source quantifies the operator-required share for CVBs over five years.
AI-powered search is already reducing traditional website traffic, but its effect on convention wins, partner value, and public mandates is not measured.
Large convention destinations and small visitor-information bureaus have different exposure to self-service substitution.
o.change_evidence: Buyer and funder research measuring planner use of CVBs versus AI and platforms, chatbot containment, website referral losses, convention lead sourcing, municipal renewal decisions, and which services stakeholders still require from an official operator would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Travelers and planners can increasingly obtain basic destination information, itinerary ideas, translations, and comparisons directly from platforms and AI agents. An accountable local organization remains important for official representation, convention bids, venue and hotel coordination, stakeholder access, public funding, local-data validation, crisis communication, brand stewardship, and community legitimacy. The operator role therefore contracts at the informational edge but persists in relationship-heavy and civic functions.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: Destinations International documents public-sector funding concentration, funding risk, diversified levies and assessments, and current AI-enabled capacity in destination organizations. Transformation: budgeted tax, assessment, membership, sponsorship, advertising, and contract revenue is treated as less mechanically tied to labor hours than cost-plus billing, allowing implemented benefits to be retained as capacity, faster service, broader language coverage, or operating margin, then discounted for budget reviews, procurement, stakeholder scrutiny, and mandated reinvestment. Direction of error: dedicated funding may protect retained capacity more than assumed, while nonprofit restrictions and public funders may capture nearly all savings through added deliverables or lower allocations.
caveats: No source measures the five-year share of implemented gross benefit retained by U.S. lower-middle-market CVBs.
Retention as distributable margin differs sharply from retention as additional mission capacity in nonprofit organizations.
Funding structures vary among dedicated taxes, improvement-district assessments, municipal contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants.
q.change_evidence: Contract, budget, and audited-financial cohorts showing funding formulas, renewal resets, labor expense, AI spend, service levels, unrestricted surplus, and changes in sponsor or public allocations before and after automation would materially change commercial retention.
q.rationale: Visitor and planner services are often free at use, so labor savings are not passed through transaction by transaction. Dedicated taxes and assessments can preserve an approved budget, and AI can expand output within it. Retention is limited because public and member funders scrutinize performance, 42 percent of surveyed organizations reported funding at risk within three years, and nonprofit operators may be expected to reinvest savings rather than distribute them.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Destinations International's global study and industry blog document active use of generative AI for itineraries, multilingual content, creative workflows, research, optimization, chatbots, and internal work, alongside uneven adoption and policy development. Transformation: demonstrated uses in destination organizations are translated into the portion of remaining exposed work a scaled U.S. operator can operationalize within five years after training, review, integration, brand, data, accessibility, and public-accountability constraints. Direction of error: highlighted adopters and association participants likely overstate typical readiness, while five years of product integration and the labor pressure identified in the industry study could make current examples understate eventual implementation.
caveats: The 2025 Futures Study has 537 participants across 36 countries rather than a representative U.S. six-digit sample.
The industry blog emphasizes a handful of named adopters and does not publish the survey response count or realized payroll savings.
Public records, accessibility, procurement, brand safety, and community-trust obligations can slow implementation even when tools are technically available.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after deployment audits across U.S. CVBs measuring eligible workflows, active-seat adoption, human-review minutes, factual correction rates, integration cost, multilingual quality, and realized labor hours would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: The core tools already operate in workflows central to the industry, and destination organizations report leadership mandates, staff training, chatbots, expanded language coverage, and new content formats. Implementation remains short of straight-through automation because local facts change, public brands require review, staff adoption is uneven, data quality and privacy matter, and convention or stakeholder commitments need accountable approval.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years. Transformation: the intention rate is applied only to the small eligible private subset and discounted for plans that do not close, nonmarket gifts, internal changes, public-contract novations, and ownership changes blocked by funding or change-of-control terms. Direction of error: broad employer data may overstate completed qualifying transactions, while the eligible subset's scale, contracts, and staff may make it more transferable than the average small employer.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Destination-organization governance changes, executive succession, mergers of nonprofits, and public contract rebids usually are not purchases of equity control.
The tiny eligible subset makes deal-frequency estimates especially unstable.
s5.change_evidence: Completed five-year control-transaction counts for private destination marketing and visitor-services operators from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, municipal contract disclosures, and specialist brokers, matched to an eligible-firm denominator, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A genuinely private, contract-backed destination-services operator can transfer, but stated succession intentions are not completed control deals. The dominant nonprofit, public, and board-governed structures belong in eligibility rather than being counted as acquisitions; leadership turnover and municipal rebids are not qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Marketing, information, translation, research, and reporting workflows are digitally intensive and already show practical AI adoption.
principal_weakness: Publicly funded nonprofit and board-governed structures sharply limit the pool of independently transferable firms.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 561591 as establishments marketing communities and facilities to business and leisure travelers through meeting-site assistance, travel information, maps, and local group tours, and distinguishes event organizers and government tourism offices.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022

sources.supports: Reports employment shares and wages for the parent travel-arrangement industry, including management, business operations, marketing, event planning, sales, customer service, reservation, and administrative occupations.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 561500

sources.supports: Reports evidence from 537 participants in 36 countries on destination-organization funding risk, expanding mandates, workforce needs, AI-enabled marketing, events, data, governance, authenticity, and implementation constraints.
sources.title: 2025 Destinations International Futures Study

sources.supports: Documents destination-organization use of AI-powered search adaptation, content formats, chatbots, multilingual service, internal training, monitoring, and uneven policy and staff adoption.
sources.title: How Destination Organizations Are Using AI and Measuring Its Impact

sources.supports: Reports destination-organization functions and funding models, including 92 percent of funding through public-sector channels, memberships and partnerships, tourism improvement districts, promotion activity, and local stakeholder roles.
sources.title: Destination Promotion: A Catalyst for Community Vitality

sources.supports: Reports inflation-adjusted forecasts for domestic leisure, business, and international travel, including continued but uneven real growth and prioritization of in-person business engagement.
sources.title: U.S. Travel Forecast

sources.supports: Provides the 2019-2030 forecast table for total, domestic, leisure, business, transient, group, and international travel spending.
sources.title: U.S. Travel Forecast, Spring 2026

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years and documents the survey population and intent-based nature of the measure.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Provides a concrete large-CVB example of a private nonprofit trade association governed by a board, contracted by a city, and funded by lodging tax, memberships, advertising, sponsorship, and fundraising.
sources.title: About Visit Denver

title: Convention and Visitors Bureaus
